title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Food & Agriculture
description: Curated OT/ICS cybersecurity advisories, incidents and threat intelligence from primary sources. Editorial summaries by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

_otthreatfeed.usage: Free to reuse with attribution. Credit 'Mike Holcomb & UtilSec' and link back to otthreatfeed.com and to each item's linked primary source. Summaries are editorial paraphrase - verify against the primary source before acting.

items.title: Ransomware shuts two Mackay Sugar mills at the start of crushing season — harvest disrupted for 1,300 growers
items.content_text: A ransomware attack on Mackay Sugar, Australia's second-largest sugar producer, halted milling and cane haulage at its Farleigh and Racecourse mills after striking on 10 June — at the very start of the crushing season. More than 1,300 family-owned cane farms had harvesting disrupted, and because the mills' cogeneration plant also feeds the regional grid, the outage rippled beyond the fence line. The Gentlemen ransomware group claimed the intrusion on 16 June and threatened to leak stolen data, a double-extortion pattern the crew has used against Asia-Pacific critical infrastructure since mid-2025. For food and agriculture the lesson is seasonal timing: attackers hit when a processor can least afford downtime, turning IT ransomware into lost harvest, spoiled throughput and idled growers.

items.title: Fresh extortion victims across food and ag — AC Beverage and Apex Agro listed in early July
items.content_text: Extortion trackers logged new food-and-agriculture victims in early July, including U.S. beverage-services firm AC Beverage, listed by the PEAR actor, and Texas agricultural-chemical company Apex Agro, listed by Genesis — both surfacing around 6 July. Individually these are mid-size firms, but the steady drumbeat is the point: the farm-to-table supply chain is a broad, soft target set where a single processor, distributor or input supplier going down ripples outward. For operators the takeaway is that size is no shield; basic segmentation, backups and monitored remote access matter for everyone in the chain.

items.title: FortiBleed credential theft now feeds ransomware — the remote path into processing plants
items.content_text: SOCRadar reported on 2 July that FortiBleed — a sniffer that harvested 110M+ credentials from roughly 430,000 internet-facing FortiGate firewalls since mid-June — is now driving INC Ransom and Lynx intrusions. Food and beverage processors, cold-chain operators and distributors lean on Fortinet for remote access to plant networks, refrigeration and packaging controls, and vendor links. Because credentials leaked rather than a bug being exploited, no patch helps: an attacker signs in as a trusted engineer. Assume any exposed FortiGate touching processing or cold-chain OT is compromised and rotate now.

items.title: Food and Ag-ISAC: 72 active threat actors are working the farm-to-table supply chain
items.content_text: The Food and Ag-ISAC identified 72 active threat actors — from a pool of more than 330 monitored — consistently targeting food supply chains, spanning both nation-state groups and financially motivated criminals. Russia accounts for the largest share of observed activity, followed by China, with Lazarus Group, Moonstone Sleet and APT41 among the top-scoring adversaries. The most common tradecraft is living-off-the-land using legitimate tools, plus modified malware, long persistence and stealthy exfiltration. The message for the sector: this is deliberate, sophisticated targeting, not opportunistic noise, and defenses should assume patient, stealthy adversaries.

items.title: Food and Ag-ISAC reports an 82% surge in ransomware, led by Qilin, Akira and CL0P
items.content_text: The Food and Ag-ISAC also reported an 82% year-over-year surge in ransomware against the sector, with Qilin, Akira, CL0P, Play and Lynx leading the campaigns — the same ecosystem that listed the world's largest food distributor, Sysco, on a leak site earlier in 2026. Legacy processing systems bridged to modern networks leave the sector dangerously exposed, and ransomware has repeatedly halted processing and packing lines worldwide. The practical response is unchanged but urgent: segmentation, tested backups and rehearsed manual operation.

items.title: CISA flags CVSS-10 flaws in Gardyn IoT Hub — a warning shot for precision and indoor agriculture
items.content_text: On 2 July CISA published advisory ICSA-26-183-03 for Gardyn IoT Hub, with flaws scoring up to CVSS 10 that let unauthenticated attackers take full control of Gardyn-managed devices. An exposed privileged iothubowner key (CVE-2026-13768) lets an attacker enumerate the entire fleet, run arbitrary commands on connected devices and pivot into the owner's home network, while device logs sat in a publicly listable cloud container. Gardyn is consumer indoor-growing hardware, but CISA scoped it to the food-and-agriculture sector for a reason: as precision-ag and controlled-environment platforms proliferate, cloud-managed IoT with exposed admin keys becomes a real agricultural attack surface.

items.title: June CISA cycle flags Delta and Schneider automation used in food and beverage lines
items.content_text: A June CISA advisory cycle covered vulnerabilities across Delta, Schneider Electric, Mitsubishi and other automation vendors whose controllers, drives and HMIs run food and beverage processing, packaging and bottling lines. Most items ship with vendor fixes or mitigations. For food and ag operators the discipline is to map the flagged products to your line inventory and prioritize anything internet-reachable or tied to food-safety-critical control — temperature, dosing, CIP — where an integrity failure carries safety and recall risk.

items.title: Food and Ag-ISAC broadens its threat reporting beyond ransomware — a maturing sector playbook
items.content_text: The Food and Ag-ISAC has expanded its cyber threat reporting to cover the full adversary landscape — nation-state espionage, supply-chain compromise and disruptive operations — not just ransomware, reflecting a sector that is professionalizing its intelligence sharing. For operators, ISAC membership (free to many in the sector) is among the highest-leverage moves available: sector-specific threat intel, early warning and peer benchmarking that a single processor or grower cooperative cannot generate alone. Treat information sharing as core infrastructure, not a nice-to-have.
